volumeTitle: Casina. The Casket Comedy. Curculio. Epidicus. The Two Menaechmuses
label: The Two Menaechmuses (Section 5.9.1121c - Section 5.9.1160)

sections.label: Section 5.9.1121c
sections.original: Dic mihi :
sections.translation: Tell me , were you both of the same name ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1122b
sections.original: Minime . nam mihi hoc erat ,
sections.translation: By no means ; for my name was what it is now Menaechmus ; the other they then used to call Sosicles .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1124
sections.original: Signa adgnovi , contineri quin complectar non queo .
sections.translation: I recognize the proofs , I cannot refrain from embracing him .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1125
sections.original: mi germane gemine frater , salve . ego sum Sosicles .
sections.translation: My own twin-brother , blessings on you ; I am Sosicles .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1126
sections.original: Quo modo igitur post Menaechmo nomen est factum tibi ?
sections.translation: How then was the name of Menaechmus afterwards given to you ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1127
sections.original: Postquam ad nos renuntiatum est te ⋯ et patrem esse mortuom ,
sections.translation: After word was brought to us that you ⋯ and that my father was dead , my grandfather changed it ; the name that was yours he gave to me .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1129_1130
sections.original: Credo ita esse factum ut dicis . sed mi hoc responde .
sections.translation: I believe that it did so happen as you say . But answer me this .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1129_1130b
sections.original: Roga .
sections.translation: Ask it of me .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1131
sections.original: Quid erat nomen nostrae matri ?
sections.translation: What was the name of our mother ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1131b
sections.original: Teuximarchae .
sections.translation: Teuximarcha .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1131c
sections.original: Convenit .
sections.translation: That quite agrees . ( He again embraces him .) O welcome , unhoped-for brother , whom after many years I now behold .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1133
sections.original: Frater , et tu , quém ego multis miseriis laboribus
sections.translation: And you , whom with many and anxious labours I have ever been seeking up to this time , and whom I rejoice at being found .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1135
sections.original: Hoc erat , quod haec te meretrix huius vocabat nomine :
sections.translation: It was for this reason that this Courtesan called you by his name ; she thought that you were he , I suppose , when she invited you to breakfast .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1137
sections.original: Namque edepol iussi hic mihi hodie prandium appararier ,
sections.translation: Why , faith , to-day I ordered a breakfast to be got ready here ( pointing to EROTIUM ’ S house ) for me , unknown to my wife ; a mantle which a short time since I filched from home , to her I gave it .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1139b
sections.original: Hanc , dicis , frater , pallam , quam ego habeo ?
sections.translation: Do you say , brother , that this is the mantle which I ’ m wearing ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1139c
sections.original: Haec east .
sections.translation: How did this come to you ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1140b
sections.original: Meretrix huc ad prandium
sections.translation: The Courtesan who took me here ( pointing to EROTIUM ’ S house ) to breakfast , said that I had given it to her . I breakfasted very pleasantly ; I drank and entertained myself with my mistress ; she gave me the mantle and this golden trinket .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1143
sections.original: Gaudeo edepol , si quid propter me tibi evenit boni .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I ’ m glad if any luck has befallen you on my account ; for when she invited you to her house , she supposed it to be me .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1146
sections.original: Numquid me morare quin ego liber , ut iusti , siem ?
sections.translation: Do you make any objection that I should be free as you commanded ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1147
sections.original: Optimum atque aequissimum orat , frater : fac causa mea .
sections.translation: He asks , brother , what ’ s very fair and very just . Do it for my sake .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1148
sections.original: Liber esto .
sections.translation: Be thou a free man .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1148b
sections.original: Quom tu es liber , gaudeo , Messenio .
sections.translation: I am glad , Messenio , that you are free .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1149_1150
sections.original: Sed meliorest opus auspicio , ut liber perpetuo siem .
sections.translation: Why , better auspices [*] were required that I should be free for life . ⋯

sections.label: Section 5.9.1151
sections.original: Quoniam haec evenerunt , frater , nostra éx sententia ,
sections.translation: Since these matters , brother , have turned out to our wishes , let us both return to our native land .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1152b
sections.original: Frater , faciam , ut tu voles .
sections.translation: Brother , I ’ ll do as you wish . I ’ ll have an auction here , and sell whatever I have . In the meantime , brother , let ’ s now go in-doors .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1154b
sections.original: Fiat .
sections.translation: Be it so .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1154c
sections.original: Scitin quid ego vos rogo ?
sections.translation: Do you know what I ask of you ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1155
sections.original: Quid ?
sections.translation: What ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1155b
sections.original: Praeconium mi ut detis .
sections.translation: To give me the place of auctioneer .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1155c
sections.original: Dabitur .
sections.translation: It shall be given you .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1155d
sections.original: Ergo nunciam
sections.translation: Would you like the auction , then , to be proclaimed at once ? For what day ?

sections.label: Section 5.9.1156b
sections.original: Equidem die septimi .
sections.translation: On the seventh day hence .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1157
sections.original: Auctio fiet Menaechmi mane sane septimi .
sections.translation: An auction of the property of Menaechmus will certainly take place on the morning of the seventh day hence . His slaves , furniture , house , and farms , will be sold . All will go for whatever they ’ ll fetch at ready money prices .

sections.label: Section 5.9.1160
sections.original: venibit uxor quoque etiam , si quis emptor venerit .
sections.translation: His wife , too , will be sold as well , if any purchaser shall come . I think that by the entire sale Menaechmus will hardly get fifty hundred thousand [*] sesterces . ( To the SPECTATORS .) Now , Spectators , fare you well , and give us loud applause [*] .
